data.title: Lazard Emerging Markets Equity Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Equity markets in the developing world, as measured by the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, rose 4.7% in the fourth quarter, as risk appetites remained largely resilient amid investor enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI) and despite tariff risks and geopolitical risks.

Latin America was the best-performing region, climbing 8.2%. Stock markets in Chile and Peru gained in sympathy with the rise in commodity prices. Brazil's stock market rose, thanks to interest rate cuts locally and in the US. Argentina's stock market jumped on news that libertarian President Javier Milei won a landslide victory in midterm elections, handing him a mandate to continue pursuing his radical overhaul of the country's economy.

The region encompassing emerging Europe, the Middle East, and Africa (EMEA) was the worst-performing region, rising 4.0%. Stock markets in Poland, Hungary, and the Czech Republic rose, as investors began to anticipate better economic prospects and a possibility of a peace agreement between Russia and Ukraine. South Africa's stock market rose in sympathy with the rise in the price of gold.

Emerging Asia also underperformed, advancing 4.5%. Stock markets in global technology hubs Korea and Taiwan rose on optimism about the demand outlook for chips, the flagship industry of both countries, despite wavering sentiment in November. China's stock market fell on concerns about the country's economic outlook.

Information technology, materials, and energy were the best-performing sectors in the period. Consumer discretionary, health care and communication services were the worst-performing sectors.

(Index returns were measured net of taxes and in US dollar terms.)

Portfolio Review

In the fourth quarter, the Lazard Emerging Markets Equity Portfolio rose in absolute terms and outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. (Portfolio returns were measured net of fees and in US dollar terms.)

Helped

  • Stock selection in the consumer discretionary and financials sectors boosted relative performance, as did stock selection in China and Taiwan.
  • Higher-than-benchmark exposures to the financials sector and to South Africa and Hungary contributed.
  • Lower-than-benchmark exposures to the consumer discretionary and communication services and to China and Saudi Arabia were beneficial to relative performance.
  • Shares of SK Hynix (HXSCF) (3.9% Portfolio weighting) traded higher after the Korea-based chipmaker reported record third-quarter earnings, anchored by high AI-related demand for its chips.
  • Shares of ASE Technology (ASX) (2.7% Portfolio weighting) rose after the Taiwan-based chip packaging and testing provider reported better-than-expected third-quarter results, anchored by robust AI-fueled demand for its services. Shares received an additional boost after the company issued an upbeat outlook due to the AI-related demand.
  • Traders bid up shares of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) (4.6% Portfolio weighting) after the Taiwan-based contract chipmaker reported better-than-expected third-quarter results. Shares received an additional boost after the company issued a positive revision to its 2025 sales growth forecast to the mid-thirties.
  • Shares of Wiwynn Corp (WYWNF) (1.8% Portfolio weighting) climbed after the Taiwan-based cloud IT infrastructure provider reported better-than-expected quarterly results, highlighted by robust revenue growth and margin expansion, driven by strong demand from hyperscale data-center clients and AI-related server investments.
  • Shares of Yageo (YGPBY) (0.8% Portfolio weighting) advanced after the Taiwan-based chip resistors maker issued an upbeat guidance, with solid domestic demand across all segments and good restocking demand from global distributors.

Hurt

  • Stock selection in the energy sector undercut relative performance, as did stock selection in Brazil and Korea.
  • Higher-than-benchmark exposures to the consumer staples sector and Greece detracted.
  • Lower-than-benchmark exposures to the information technology and materials sectors and to Taiwan and Korea were detrimental to relative performance.
  • Shares of e-commerce giant Alibaba Group (BABA) (1.1% Portfolio weighting) fell on profit-taking after recent strong stock price appreciation.
  • Shares of Lenovo (LNVGY) (0.9% Portfolio weighting) fell on concerns about the China-based personal computer and data center equipment maker's earning outlook due to concerns that the rising costs of memory would hurt margins and demand in 2026.
  • Taiwan-based contract electronics manufacturer Quanta Computer (QUCCF) (1.6% Portfolio weighting) saw its stock price fall on concerns about margin pressure, which offset the normalization in server and notebook shipments and ongoing exposure to AI-related hardware demand.
  • Shares of GlobalWafers (GWALF) (0.9% Portfolio weighting) receded on concerns about the Taiwan-based silicon wafer maker's earnings outlook due to weaker near-term demand for wafers and delayed customer orders.
  • The stock price of Novatek Microelectronics (NOVKY) (0.7% Portfolio weighting) fell after the Taiwan-based display-driver integrated circuits supplier reported a drop in net profits.

Outlook

Most market fundamentals remain fluid based upon significant geopolitical and trade risks as well as valuations. Our expectations will vary based upon these issues, but, at this juncture, we remain reasonably constructive on emerging markets as long as global growth remains balanced.

Lazard Emerging Markets Equity Portfolio (LZEMX)

Important Information

Please consider a fund's investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses carefully before investing. For more complete information about The Lazard Funds, Inc. and current performance, you may obtain a prospectus or summary prospectus by calling 800-823-6300 or going to Lazard Asset Management . Read the prospectus or summary prospectus carefully before you invest. The prospectus and summary prospectus contain investment objectives, risks, charges, expenses, and other information about the Portfolio and The Lazard Funds that may not be detailed in this document. The Lazard Funds are distributed by Lazard Asset Management Securities LLC.

The performance quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. The current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. An investor may obtain performance data current to the most recent month-end online at Lazard Asset Management . The investment return and principal value of the Portfolio will fluctuate; an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost.

Different share classes may have different returns and different investment minimums.

Please click here for standardized returns: Lazard Emerging Markets Equity Portfolio

Information and opinions presented have been obtained or derived from sources believed by Lazard Asset Management LLC or its affiliates ("Lazard") to be reliable. Lazard makes no representation as to their accuracy or completeness. All opinions expressed herein are as of the published date and are subject to change.

Allocations and security selection are subject to change.

Mention of these securities should not be considered a recommendation or solicitation to purchase or sell the securities. It should not be assumed that any investment in these securities was, or will prove to be, profitable, or that the investment decisions we make in the future will be profitable or equal to the investment performance of securities referenced herein. There is no assurance that any securities referenced herein are currently held in the portfolio or that securities sold have not been repurchased. The securities mentioned may not represent the entire portfolio.

Equity securities will fluctuate in price; the value of your investment will thus fluctuate, and this may result in a loss. Securities in certain non-domestic countries may be less liquid, more volatile, and less subject to governmental supervision than in one's home market. The values of these securities may be affected by changes in currency rates, application of a country's specific tax laws, changes in government administration, and economic and monetary policy.

Emerging markets securities carry special risks, such as less developed or less efficient trading markets, a lack of company information, and differing auditing and legal standards. The securities markets of emerging markets countries can be extremely volatile; performance can also be influenced by political, social, and economic factors affecting companies in these countries.

The MSCI Emerging Markets Index is a free-float-adjusted market capitalization index that is designed to measure equity market performance in the global emerging markets. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index consists of emerging markets country indices including: Brazil, Chile, China, Colombia, Czech Republic, Egypt, Greece, Hungary, India, Indonesia, Korea, Malaysia, Mexico, Peru, Philippines, Poland, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Taiwan, Thailand, Turkey, and United Arab Emirates. The index is unmanaged and has no fees. One cannot invest directly in an index.

Certain information included herein is derived by Lazard in part from an MSCI index or indices (the "Index Data"). However, MSCI has not reviewed this product or report, and does not endorse or express any opinion regarding this product or report or any analysis or other information contained herein or the author or source of any such information or analysis. MSCI makes no express or implied warranties or representations and shall have no liability whatsoever with respect to any Index Data or data derived therefrom.

Certain information contained herein constitutes "forward-looking statements" which can be identified by the use of forward-looking terminology such as "may," "will," "should," "expect," "anticipate," "target," "intent," "continue," or "believe," or the negatives thereof or other variations thereon or comparable terminology. Due to various risks and uncertainties, actual events may differ materially from those reflected or contemplated in such forward-looking statements.

Disclosure:

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Vornado Realty: Reviewing The 7%+ Preferreds After The Latest Earnings
data.description:
In the following lines, we will take a look at a company that we have reviewed before - Vornado Realty Trust (VNO) and its financial instruments (you can see our last article here). VNO announced its fourth quarter, and we want to find out if there is a difference in financial metrics and instruments. So, let's get started...

VNO in Details

Vornado Realty Trust's current market capitalization is around $6.57 billion. For the 4th quarter of 2025, the company has close to $15.5 billion in total assets, and the total debt is ~ $7.94 billion. The preferred equity of VNO is approximately $1.18 billion. EBITDA is close to $788 million. The portfolio is composed of:

- Оffice properties: 20 million square feet

- Retail properties: 2.4 million square feet

- Marketplaces: 1.6 million square feet

- Residential properties: over 1600 rental apartments

- Studios and other mixed-use facilities

Financial Metrics

Using the information from the earnings, we will calculate some important financial metrics for the company:

  • Asset Yield ((total revenue - operating expenses + depreciation and amortization) / total assets): 5.08%.
  • Asset Coverage Ratio (total assets/debt): 196%.
  • Operating Expenses/Total Revenue (excluding depreciation and amortization): 57%.
  • Interest Expenses/Debt: 4.16%.
  • Rental Revenue: 10%

We also calculate market-adjusted asset yield to be more accurate. Therefore, we will need a price-to-book value, which is, at the time of writing, 1.29. We replace the book value of equity with its market value by applying the current Price/Book. From here, we can calculate the market-adjusted yield and the market-adjusted asset coverage ratio.

  • Market Adjusted Asset Yield: 4.5%.
  • Market Adjusted Asset Coverage ratio: 220.4%.
  • Market Adjusted Rental Revenue (lease revenue/market-adjusted total assets) gross on leasing. : 8.9%
  • Expected AFFO Yield: 5.05%. VNO is among the highest forward P/AFFOs in the sector.

Credibility

The credit rating of the company hasn't changed from its last quarter. Moody's rates it with a "Ba1" credit rating, Fitch rates it with "BB+" and S&P rates it with an "BBB-" credit rating:

We will try to check VNO's credibility, using Moody's approach as always:

- The gross assets of VNO are close to $19.7 billion, and this is equivalent to an "A1" credit rating.

- The unencumbered assets are around 94%, or equivalent to an "A1" credit rating again.

- The secured debt is close to 25% of the gross assets, or this is equivalent to a "Ba2" credit rating.

- The total debt + preferred stock is approximately 46% of the gross assets, or this is a "Baa3" credit rating.

- Net debt to EBITDA: We use the total debt here, and with an EBITDA of $788 million, the ratio is close to 11.6, or a "Ca3" credit rating.

- Fixed charge coverage: EBITDA covers interest and preferred stock dividends at a rate of close to 2 times - "Ba2".

- Market positioning, asset quality, and operating environment: Baa1

The company's operations are largely focused on commercial and office properties.

- Liquidity and access to capital - The operating expenses represent about 57% of revenue, and EBITDA covers interest and preferred share dividends 2 times. Here, the credit rating is equivalent to a "B3."

After calculating all ratios based on market-adjusted characteristics, we obtain a Ba1 credit rating. For the preferreds, we have to go two notches lower and give them a B3.

Preferred Stocks

VNO has four preferred stocks (VNO.PR.L), (VNO.PR.M), (VNO.PR.N), and (VNO.PR.O). All four trade below par currently and have yields to worst of around 7.34-7.47%.

We will look at and use for comparison the highest-yielding VNO.PR.L. The preferred stock has a close to 7.47% current yield. It was issued on 01/16/2013 with a coupon rate of 5.4% and spread to the treasury yield of around 2.65%, while currently this spread is around 2.77%. The call date of the stock has passed, so it is callable anytime. Below you can see its potential current yields vs. prices:

And in the next picture is shown where VNO's stocks are staying among its peers:

Below is shown the REIT OTC bond sector:

The highest yield in the group is around 6.78% and a slightly higher credit rating, so we'll add a 0.2% premium, or 7% yield to maturity. This makes the YTM nearly 0.5% lower than VNO.PR.O's yield. If VNO.PR.O were trading at a yield equivalent to this bond, its price would be around $19.25, representing roughly 6% upside potential from the current price.

OTC Bonds

Above are shown VNO's over-the-counter bonds. The higher yield to maturity is close to 5.4%, with a spread to the treasury yield of around 1.3%. The bonds are rated with a "Ba1" credit rating by Moody's.

Conclusion

VNO once again presents itself with remarkably stable financial and credit indicators. The preferred stocks offer current yields above the sector's average, while credit risk remains moderate.

data.title: Suzano: Expecting Resumed Deleveraging In 2026
data.description:
Suzano S.A. (SUZ) reported the other day, and markets liked it. There was sequential improvement in the markets in terms of pricing on top of considerably more volume thanks to Cerrado and also lower average pulp costs, also thanks to the lower-cost Cerrado footprint contributing to the mix. Improvements in the market, otherwise a little bit on the down, also seem to be materialising as we were picking up on in our last coverage. The next year we expect around 14% FCFY just based on FY 2025 figures, which we believe will be easily on higher volumes and the slight improvement in prices towards the end of the year. Prices have been improving since then as well, and there are a few additional tailwinds that support this price increase into 2026 for pulp.

Results

Some of the salient points in terms of the business' development are that there are revocations of licenses in Indonesia for plantations to produce timbre eventually usable as wood chips and fibre for pulp production. Annual production is around 50 million tonnes globally and the amount affected is already 150k based on curtailments by one producer and could amount to more. With a commodity like this, even a couple hundred bps in change of supply matters. Price increases in pulp are being pushed; the $538 per tonne in the slides is already a backward looking figure. Pulp demand was up in China. The Indonesian revocations are concentrating demand on Vietnamese wood chips, which are raising those prices and affecting costs for Chinese and Japanese manufacturers and others in Asia. Cerrado is one of the most cost effective assets in the world, so higher cash costs for competitors are a good thing and are allowing price increases to be pushed onto customers. The pulp demand recovery in China is also a decent economic indicator, and paper and board production was up 17% in China, meaning end markets aren't getting worse.

For paper and packaging, price increases are happening in export markets as well.

Onto the financials: the Cerrado project is finally wrapped up in terms of CAPEX. CAPEX overall is expected to fall 3 billion BRL into the next year as Cerrado works get lapped.

Meanwhile, operating cash generation, which is net of sustaining CAPEX, was 13 billion BRL in 2025. In 2026, extraordinary CAPEX is supposed to be around 3.6 billion BRL, meaning around 10 billion BRL in FCF thanks to the end of Cerrado's works as well. Cerrado in the mix is also reducing pulp cash costs by a little less than 5% as it's lower cost than existing assets as it comes into force.

Bottom Line

10 billion BRL on around 70 billion BRL in market cap is a considerable FCFY of around 14%. That's a great yield as prices begin to move in the right direction. Deleveraging is the main capital allocation priority. With something up to 10 billion BRL going into net debt reduction with around 65 billion BRL in net debt, deleveraging is around 14% per year. We think the lower risk of financial distress for a commodity company will also increase appraisal of the assets too, so equity is positioned to gain here far ahead of that. It's a great proposition, and Suzano has some of the most competitive assets in the world. Remains a fixture of our portfolio on the deleveraging and eventual dividend angle, which we expect to be quite high on 14% FCF currently, even on limited payout ratios. With the markets bottoming out in pulp, we think that assuming last year's cash generation forward is conservative enough.

data.title: Affirm: Inflection Point From 'Story Stock' To GAAP Profit Machine Is Here
data.description:
Affirm Holdings, Inc. (AFRM) has seen great volatility as of late. This is despite the company posting strong results, with the most important being sustained and increasing GAAP profitability. The company’s incremental operating margins are impressive and bolster my view of the long-term profitability outlook. The stock valuation appears more de-risked than in years past, especially considering the refreshed financial picture. I am upgrading the stock to a "Strong Buy" rating.

AFRM Stock Price

I last covered AFRM in November, where I upgraded the stock to a "Buy" because I viewed it as a new leader. The stock has cratered 25% since.

That volatility has de-risked the valuation premium, offering an attractive opportunity for an upgrade.

AFRM Stock Key Metrics

AFRM is a leader in the buy now, pay later industry. Besides being a well-known and popular name with consumers, the company has historically also resonated with investors due to management’s beat-and-raise style. In the most recent quarter, the company delivered gross merchandise volume ("GMV") of $13.8 billion, crushing guidance of between $13 billion and $13.3 billion.

The company has seen sustained hyper-growth in its direct-to-consumer business, led by its Affirm card. I view the Affirm card as being crucial in helping to earn customer loyalty, as it helps differentiate itself from the numerous other BNPL operators.

AFRM has seen its strongest growth in 0% APR transactions. This is an important detail that many bears might be overlooking. I have heard (and at times, made) the argument that BNPL is simply a bad financing option for lower credit scores. I think this misses the whole point. Through 0% offers, AFRM enables merchants to offer promotions without discounting on price. This, in theory, helps retailers avoid the trap of continuous discounting. One could view AFRM then as being similar to online advertising in that it merely earns a sort of “commission” on sales (but I could understand if the reader thinks that I have taken this too far).

I was surprised to see AFRM show 42% YoY growth in active merchants, accelerating considerably even accounting for seasonality. It is important for these BNPL operators to rapidly grow their presence.

AFRM generated 30% YoY revenue growth to $1.123 billion, crushing guidance of between $1.03 billion and $1.06 billion.

Revenue less transaction costs grew at a comparable 29% YoY rate. RLTC and revenues do not always grow in tandem, but the company has seen some stabilization in provisions.

The company generated its third consecutive quarter of positive GAAP operating income. I would have expected the consistent profitability to help it reduce volatility in valuations, but therein lies the opportunity.

I should note, however, that delinquencies have trended higher than each of the prior years. Investors should keep a close eye on this, as delinquencies have been rising in recent years.

The company ended the quarter with $1.1 billion in net cash, which, with the GAAP profitability, represents a stellar balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management noted that the upcoming quarters will be suffering from tough comparables as an enterprise merchant has brought their BNPL solutions in-house.

Nonetheless, management has still guided the company to see up to 27.7% YoY and 24.4% YoY revenue growth in the third and fourth quarters, respectively.

On the conference call, management cited strength in the asset-backed securities market, noting that they were able to price a deal with “a spread of under 100 basis points,” which is something that they hadn’t done “since 2021.” This is an important note, as the company was a pandemic darling and interest rates were lower in 2021. The strong ABS showing indicates that the credit markets have validated AFRM’s business model. Management continues to target the 0% market, noting that it is successful mainly “because it's so easy to understand.” Management also noted their strategic priority of being a “default” payment option with retailers, meaning that their logo would automatically come up at checkout. I view AFRM as being similar to the early days of PayPal (PYPL), one in which there is great value in being a first-mover and taking as much market share as possible, with consumers likely to choose the BNPL option that they have already used before.

Is AFRM Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At recent prices, AFRM traded at just 4x forward sales. That’s a far cry from the 7x sales multiple that it had previously traded at in September. Consensus estimates call for the company to sustain robust growth over the coming years.

The company has rapid improvements in profit margins, which forms the basis of my bullish thesis at the moment. As previously seen, the company generated a solid 10% GAAP operating margin and 30% non-GAAP operating margin in the quarter. That improvement comes on the backs of having generated an astounding 47% incremental GAAP operating margin in the quarter.

Perhaps one could quibble about whether the reduction in S&M is sustainable every year, but I would expect the company to realize operational efficiencies in other cost segments as well. But even if we exclude the gains in S&M, the company still generated a 33% incremental GAAP operating margin. I have previously assumed that the company will hit 20% net margins over the long term. Between the company’s third consecutive quarter of GAAP profitability (including again a 10% GAAP operating margin) and the high incremental margins, I am growing of the view that 20% might prove too conservative. Let’s stick with 20%. That places the stock at just 20x long-term earnings. Against 24% forward growth, that is a less than 1x price-to-earnings growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’). I could see AFRM re-rating higher, but the stock looks priced for at least 15% to 20% annual returns even if one assumes some multiple compression along the way.

So why is the stock trading at more reasonable valuations? I do not think it is due to the proposed 10% cap on credit card rates, as I would expect any reduced demand for credit cards to be a tailwind for BNPL operators like AFRM. I instead think the reason is due to concerns that the economy may be slowing down. Perhaps investors are also afraid that AI might lead to widespread layoffs, which might hurt consumer spending. My crystal ball is not telling me anything, and AFRM is likely to see volatility in this scenario. It is highly possible that consensus estimates prove too optimistic in the near term. That said, I view BNPL as more than just a trend and something that is absolutely here to stay, especially due to the aforementioned marketing benefits. The company’s GAAP profitability and net cash balance sheet may help insulate it from any future recessions. I can see the stock working out even if long-term growth comes in at just 15% on average over the next few years (though I find that unlikely). I am upgrading the stock to a strong buy, but note that the market is likely to still view this as being highly sensitive to a weak economy, meaning that volatility cannot be avoided.

AFRM Stock Risks

The economy might slip into a recession. AFRM might see elevated delinquencies alongside lower GMV. It is possible that AI disrupts the industry, perhaps allowing an upstart to offer a cheaper product. AFRM is not yet that profitable on a GAAP basis, and thus the stock is likely to sustain great volatility. More big retailers might bring BNPL operations in-house, perhaps helped by vibe coding.

AFRM Stock Conclusion

The market continues to view AFRM as being a high-risk, high-reward name. The market is not giving the company enough credit for the sustained top-line growth as well as the rapidly improving GAAP profit margins. Growth rates might face volatility in a recession, but I expect AFRM to remain a leader in the resilient BNPL industry. AFRM is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am upgrading the stock to a "Strong Buy" rating.

data.title: Amazon's Biggest Investments Ever Could Lead To Its Quietest Stock Year
data.description:
Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) guided ~$200b in 2026 capex, with a majority of the spending going to AWS for AI - one of the largest single-year investments in corporate history. This thesis looks at the impact of this spend on the stock and finds the initial correction in share prices to be in line with what has been historically observed through large buildout phases - that is, a period of capped upside for the next few quarters.

Capex spending has not always been punished across the tech leadership in the current AI race, but Amazon's case faces pressure due to cash conversion cycle delays and the extent of FCF impact. That should keep Amazon on Hold, despite Q4 numbers otherwise showing encouraging trends. The long-term Buy thesis remains intact, and Amazon will bounce back strongly once utilization ramps up, but it may go through a prolonged consolidation in the near term. I retain my Hold rating, expecting better time-adjusted and absolute entry points in the future.

FCF Impact A Concern

Amazon has been through a build phase in the past around 2016-17, 2021-22, and now this phase that stretches from 2024. These periods of rising capex have meant pressure on the FCF. The stock (and valuation) has often stayed under pressure too, doing most of the running in the harvesting periods between these cycles.

The magnitude of capex involved in the current cycle is far higher. Capex has been surging from ~$50b in 2023 to ~$80b in 2024 and ~$125b for 2025 (collated through earnings call transcripts data and guidance). And the relentless uptick continues, even bigger, in 2026. These are unprecedented levels of capex and potentially the most prolonged - of course, in response to a once-in-a-generation AI opportunity.

Through this capex cycle, Amazon has been going through an expected FCF decline, with the most recent TTM FCF reported as ~$11.2b. The operating cash flow reported in 2025 is ~$139b. Most of the conversion from operating cash flow to free cash flow is absorbed by ~$125b in capex (lower impact of working capital). With around $75b increase expected in 2026, and assuming similar FCF conversion dynamics and ~20% operating cash flow growth, similar to 2025 (projected hence to ~$166b), Amazon is likely moving toward a negative FCF regime again (anywhere between -$30b and -$50b).

This is where the near-term story comes under pressure - the extent of FCF decline and a potential drag on AWS operating margins too. Depreciation on data center assets could span years, so operating income absorbs non-cash expenses until utilization peaks. Management also cautioned against straight-line expectations around operating margins through a heavy build phase.

Naturally, the markets reacted negatively to the capex news because it means the free cash flow gets pulled forward materially. Near-term margins will compress, and there will be delayed payoff at a time when markets are more uncomfortable with underwriting long-duration growth.

Why Capex Matters For Amazon

Heavy capital spending by itself is not the primary determinant of share prices. It hasn't been in this AI race cycle of the past couple of years. What matters is how quickly the spend translates into visible revenue and earnings. Markets have differentiated capex spending on this cash conversion timing variable across various stocks. And the capex size is just one variable there.

Google recently announced a comparable and substantial capex plan for 2026 - ~$180b at the midpoint. This is certainly still a drag, but the markets appear to be more supportive so far, even after a strong rally in the last 6 months, whereas Amazon has been consolidating before and still corrected significantly. This is potentially because Google's core search and YouTube advertising businesses generate substantial operating cash flow even as AI and data center investments rise, keeping free cash flow relatively healthy.

On the other hand, markets have not entirely overlooked the capex drag on Microsoft and Meta Platforms. Both companies have seen periods of investor caution and increased focus on growth and margins, keeping the share prices in check. Tesla is a different story, where the narrative is strengthened and FCF is traditionally a less important predictor of share prices, but even there I feel share prices are supported due to the current substantial build phase at best; upside is again pressured.

Overall, capex matters more for Amazon at this point because it has already been under a lot of free cash flow pressure in the recent past, and the absolute damage in cash flow is potentially the most heightened. The difference is that Amazon is entering this next leg of the investment cycle from a much weaker free cash flow position. This makes the near-term financial optics more strained and delays the point at which markets begin to reward the long-term earnings potential being built today. The fact that the investments are in the longer cash conversion end of this spectrum also makes it a bigger factor for Amazon. Most of the spend is expected to go toward building data centers and compute infrastructure - revenue and cash flow come in much later after utilization ramps.

Amazon also reacts differently because of its structure. Amazon’s business mix includes capital-intensive logistics operations too, along with the cloud services, which are also infrastructure-heavy. Both require ongoing reinvestment and generate higher depreciation compared to an advertising-driven business model. As a result, margins and free cash flow tend to be more volatile during build phases.

This Time Is Different

The last time FCF turned significantly negative was in 2022. This also coincided with a widespread equity market downturn and, crucially, was a period of overbuilding alongside slowing demand (after the post-pandemic highs). So, while Amazon corrected significantly by ~50% in that negative FCF regime, this time is quite different. Demand is accelerating, and AI monetization prospects reduce the chances of overbuilding. That is why I expect the share price reaction to be muted versus 2022, mostly balancing the drag from the capex cycle.

The long-term thesis also appears less uncertain. Amazon is broadly betting on AI workload and cloud workload growth to structurally rise for the next decade from here, and that secular push is fundamentally supported. Demand concerns are not the gating variable in valuing Amazon right now. Signals are structurally strong, as reported in Q4 2025. Growth was described as supply constrained. 3.9 GWs were added in the past 12 months. The AWS backlog stands at $244b, up ~40% YoY. What Amazon is building should re-accelerate AWS revenue as AI workloads keep scaling, but payoffs are multi-year and back-end loaded.

This time around, core operating trends also remain solid. AWS revenue grew 24% YoY in Q4 to a $142b annualized run rate, the fastest growth in 13 quarters. Advertising also posted solid growth of ~22% YoY. North America retail operating margin improved to 9% vs. 8% YoY despite continued fulfillment investment. The revenue trajectory is expected to remain robust in 2026. As more capacity comes online, AWS could keep growing at current average run rates of ~24%, adding $30-35 billion in revenue annually. Even assuming operating margins mature toward 30% (from ~35% currently), that's about $9b added as incremental operating income per year as the build cycle matures.

So overall, the long-term thesis stays intact for patient capital. The Amazon story is definitely not looking broken, although it may remain capped as the markets acknowledge the long-dated risks and digest the more visible near-term earnings and cash flow impacts. The risks to the long-duration benefits here are lower-than-expected long-term AI demand and lower margins later due to pricing pressure from competition. For the immediate future, though, I expect Amazon to stay range-bound and offer better entry points as the build cycle matures and after a period of time correction. When Amazon starts rerating after such a phase, it is usually a substantial move - so the costs of waiting for a better entry point (in terms of missing out entirely) do not outweigh the benefits of near-term consolidation and risk of corrections, in my view.

data.title: Performance Insights - Monthly Report: February 2026
data.description:
Equities look past geopolitical risks; market leadership remains outside the US.

Oil rose in January on multiple supply shocks; gold and silver saw a correction.

However, the growth and inflation picture remained supportive of risk assets.

Source: Author

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ARKO Petroleum: Not Fueling Up The Tank Here
data.description:
Shares of ARKO Petroleum Corp. (APC) have been trading flat to slightly down after trading opened for this fuel distributor, spun off from Arko Corp.

I can understand this cautious reaction by investors, as dependency and a challenging long-term outlook do not create compelling reasons to award high multiples to the shares.

Even as shares trade down a bit soon after a softer pricing action, this modest discount is not enticing enough for me to become upbeat, as I am more interested in learning more about the parent company.

Fuel Distribution

Arko Petroleum is a fuel distribution firm active in North America. Actually, the company claims in its filing documents that it is one of the largest wholesale fuel distributors. Formed by its parent company Arko Corp. (ARKO), which hereby breaks up this business from its core convenience retail business.

Following this separation, the company is primarily a fee-based distributor to supply fuel to stations of Arko Corp. and to third-party operators under long-term contracts. These typically have a duration of about 10 years, with weighted average remaining lease terms seen at just over 5 years here.

The company distributes no less than 2.1 billion gallons of fuel in 2024. Note that these are giant volumes but just represent about a percent of the US market, with nearly 195 billion gallons of fuel used in 2024.

The company operates under three separate units. The GPMP is the unit that sells and supplies fuel to Arko Cor wholesale, involving similar services provided to third-party dealers and bulk buyers, while fleet fueling focuses on other unstaffed cardlock commercial and municipal customers. To put some numbers to this, this involves over 1,100 Arko Corp retail sites, over 2,000 third-party gas stations, and nearly 300 unstaffed cardlock locations.

Generally, these locations are found in the eastern part of the country, proving a dense network, as the relation with Arko Corp is a bit special, of course. In fact, following the offering, the former parent company will still own a majority stake in Arko Petroleum, making this a controlled business. However, Arko Corp has an incentive to keep a good relationship, if only for the sake of its valuation and stake in Arko Petroleum.

Valuation & IPO Thoughts

Arko Petroleum aimed to sell 10.5 million shares in a preliminary price range between $18 and $20 per share, with pricing set at the lower end of the range. All the shares were sold by the company, which ended up selling 11.1 million shares to meet its $200 million gross proceeds target.

With some 46 million shares outstanding following the offering, the company is awarded an $828 million equity valuation at the offer price. The company operates with a net debt load of $356 million as of the third quarter, a number seen coming down substantially following $200 million in gross proceeds. This mostly boils down to a near billion-dollar enterprise valuation.

Revenues in this business do not tell much given the volatility in fuel prices, as a $7.1 billion revenue number in 2022 fell just below $7.0 billion in 2023, falling below $6.4 billion in 2024. Operating profits were very much more stable, reported at over $92 million, $89 million, and $92 million in these years, respectively.

For the first nine months of 2025, the performance has been lagging a bit, with low fuel prices not helping, as revenues year-to-date were reported just over $4.2 billion, trending below $6 billion per annum. Operation profits of $64 million for the nine-month period lagged the similar time in 2024 by about eight million.

If we extrapolate the numbers, the business generated about $85 million in operating profits here. With the business operating with about $170 million in pro forma net debt, applying realistic interest rates on that and statutory tax rates around 25%, I see net earnings potential in the mid-fifty millions, equal to about $1.25 per share.

Note that there would be a small upside to this, as fuel margins to third parties averaged over fourteen cents per gallon in 2024. Margins with Arko Corp were stuck around 5 cents, with this fixed margin being hiked to 6 cents as of the start of this year.

Based on this logic, shares trade at 14-15 times earnings at $18 per share, as net debt is far from an issue with EBITDA trending near $140 million each year here, as the leverage ratio just tops 1 times.

Concluding Thoughts

Frankly, a mid-teens earnings multiple looks reasonable, as this is a competitive business with long-term concerns. Of course, electrification is a key concern, eventually. While actual fuel consumption in the US has been rather stable (absent the pandemic), it has been coming down on a per-person basis, thanks to electrification but also increased efficiencies. For 2025, volumes of the business are seen down by mid-single digits to about 2.0 billion gallons.

Other concerns include thin margins, stiff competition, seasonality, and, of course, a reliance on Arko Corp. This client makes up 42% of revenues in the first nine months of the year, although its margins are much lower, with a lower profit contribution seen here. One of the benefits is that the business generally works with cost-plus arrangements, creating few margin risks (provided that no contracts are lost, of course).

However, all this has taken place in a mainly lower fuel price environment, as a shock to the oil market could really impact demand, as oil has been relatively mute in a largely inflationary environment.

Moreover, other peers and competitors are often smaller, but many of them are part of larger oil, transportation, and energy companies, making them not directly comparable.

For now the multiples appear fair, but the organic growth prospects for this business are difficult to find given the electrification in the economy and transportation. The business is furthermore reliant on the relationship with its parent, which is not the biggest in my view, given that it remains a large shareholder and its margins are subpar already.

All of this leaves me to conclude that the fair multiples are not enough to get upbeat, but that a discount must be seen before real appeal is seen. This makes me cautious to get involved here but, frankly, quite interested to learn the pro forma impact on Arko Corp.

data.title: Arhaus: The Price Is Finally Right (Upgrade)
data.description:
To be honest with you, I am not a huge fan of the home furnishings market. It is intensely competitive, and margins have a tendency to be slim. In the past, I have been neutral or negative on many companies that operate in this space. One such example can be seen by looking at Arhaus, Inc. (ARHS), a business that operates specifically as a provider of home furnishings. Its vertical business model empowers it to design and produce furniture, outdoor products, lighting products, textiles, and decor. It is a huge industry, worth around $100 billion annually if we focus on the United States alone. But in my previous article about the business, published in September of last year, I said that I needed more clarity on revenue and profits before being anything other than "Neutral."

In addition to uncertainty associated with the economy and tariffs, I decided to reaffirm the business as a "Hold" candidate. Since then, the stock has pulled back some, dropping 4.5%, while the S&P 500 is up 3%. Even with that decline, however, the stock is up 68.7% since I first rated it a "Hold" back in March 2022. That is a bit above the 59.5% increase that the market has experienced. Looking at the data today, I see that the trading multiples of the company have come down. Management expects continued growth for 2025 in its entirety. And even though economic concerns remain problematic, especially since I believe that the United States is heading toward a recession, the stock is now priced at levels that I think make a bit of sense.

Because of this, I believe that upgrading the company to a soft "Buy" is the right move. But this could change as new data comes in. Soon, that will be the case. In fact, on Feb. 26, management is expected to announce financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Although revenue is expected to increase, earnings per share should contract some. But assuming that this is more of a blip on the radar than a sign of worse times to come, I would argue that the company still justifies a cautious upgrade.

I'm More Comfortable With Arhaus

The newest data that investors have regarding Arhaus covers the third quarter of the company’s 2025 fiscal year. By all accounts, this was a good quarter for the company. Revenue jumped 8%, from $319.1 million to $344.6 million. This jump in revenue was driven largely by an increase in the number of showrooms that the company had in operation from 101 to 103. I know that doesn't sound like much, but that alone added $14.7 million of additional revenue for the company. The rest of the increase, then, was the result of comparable growth of 4.1%, with demand comparable growth coming in at a very solid 7.4%. This requires some additional detail. Management chalked this up to greater demand for the goods that the company provides.

Naturally, we would expect higher revenue to translate to higher profits. And that is precisely what we saw. Net income jumped from $9.9 million to $12.2 million. But that's not all. We saw operating cash flow expand from $31.1 million to $46.1 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $37.8 million to $58.6 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $23.1 million to $31.2 million.

The chart above shows financial performance for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Again, the rise in the number of showrooms did a lot of heavy lifting here. However, comparable revenue jumped 4.5%, while demand comparable sales grew by 2.8%. This is a fantastic showing for the company and increased not only sales but also bottom-line results across the board. Management is really working hard to keep this trend going. For instance, a big driver for the business during the third quarter was its Fall 2025 Collection, which management described as its strongest launch in the company's history.

The firm saw tremendous success here across its catalog, its website, and its showrooms. Upholstery and customization demand has been high. And designer-assisted orders have seen order values come in four times higher than those without. It is worth noting that the Fall 2025 Collection was special in large part because of the themes that management pushed. This included offering highly differentiated, artisan-crafted products, with examples including wood, stone, and more. This sense of newness that was fostered in its locations and online caused a surge in buying activity from customers. And in fact, the month of September represented the highest total demand month in the company's history. On top of this, the company continues to focus on new product launches.

Thanks to this strong performance, the company believes that revenue for 2025 will have been between $1.35 billion and $1.38 billion. For context, in 2024, revenue was $1.27 billion. The company also anticipates EBITDA of between $135 million and $145 million. For context, the previous year, it was $133.3 million. Meanwhile, adjusted operating cash flow should expand from $177.3 million to around $212.7 million. Usually, EBITDA is higher than operating cash flow. However, this is often not the case when it comes to companies that have more cash than they have debt. In fact, in the second quarter of 2025, the cash for the company exceeded debt by approximately $185.2 million. But as of the third quarter, it exceeded to the tune of $212.6 million.

Using the estimates calculated for 2025, as well as the historical results for 2024, we can see in the chart above how the stock is priced at the moment. These are pretty attractive multiples if you ask me. Normally, I like to see companies that trade in the single-digit range from a valuation standpoint. However, it is worth noting that companies in the furniture space do have a tendency to trade at relatively low multiples. In that same chart, however, you can see that only one of the firms that I compared it to is cheaper than it is on a price-to-operating cash flow basis. And when it comes to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, it is the cheapest of the group.

In addition to the stock being attractively priced on an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms, it's also a positive that analysts are optimistic. In the table below, you can see estimates for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, which management is expected to announce the results of on February 26th. If everything goes according to plan, revenue should climb from $347 million in 2024 to $351.5 million in 2025. Management has provided some estimates of their own. They expect, for instance, revenue of between $336 million and $366 million. That midpoint of $351 million is in alignment with what analysts are forecasting.

Unfortunately, profitability is likely to decline some. Analysts actually expect earnings per share to fall from $0.15 last year to $0.10 this year. This would mean a contraction in net income from $21.3 million to $14.1 million. Interestingly, this is actually near the higher end of what management itself estimated. They said that earnings should be somewhere between $6 million and $16 million. They also are forecasting EBITDA of between $25 million and $35 million. That would be below the $41.2 million reported the previous year. Although this sounds bad, it is worth noting that the expectation of weakness is largely because customers decided to pull forward demand into the third quarter of the year as opposed to the final quarter. On top of this, we have other factors at play as well. This includes the prospect of an additional $5 million of technology-related investments and the majority of tariff-related expenses hitting the company in the final quarter. Management said that, even net of mitigation activities, tariffs will cost the company about $12 million. Most of this expense will hit in the final quarter of the year.

Speaking of tariffs, management is currently forecasting the total tariff impact for 2026 to be between $50 million and $60 million. This is after factoring in benefits from vendor negotiations, the company’s decision to change around its sourcing, and certain operational efficiencies that management is pushing. Instead of the company absorbing that hit, which would represent most or all of their profits for the year, it's likely that investors should expect price hikes. New product launches have already been priced with tariffs factored in. Plus, long-term, the company is continuing to invest in domestic manufacturing. Already around 70% of its upholstery is sourced from the United States. So in all likelihood, we should expect that metric to grow.

Takeaway

Currently, I agree that uncertainty remains. However, I am not as pessimistic as I was before. I still think that we are heading toward a recession, and that could lead to some short-term pain from a fundamental standpoint. But management has done a great job proving itself. Shares are cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar companies. And while tariffs remain an issue, it's likely that price increases will absorb most of that hit. Given this combination of factors, I think that upgrading the stock to a very soft "Buy" is appropriate at this time.

data.title: Sanofi: CEO Leaves With Project Rejuvenation Unfinished, But Stock Undervalued
data.description:
Investment Overview

Sanofi (SNY), native ticker Euronext Paris: SAN, reported its Q4 and full-year earnings on January 29th, announcing net sales up >9.9% year-on-year, to €43.6bn in 2025, and net income up >12%, to €9.6bn.

Despite these apparently good results, news broke today that the French Pharma had parted company with its CEO of six years, Paul Hudson, and appointed a replacement, Belén Garijo, formerly CEO of German Pharma Merck KGaA.

In its statement, Sanofi thanked the outgoing CEO for his "valuable contributions to the transformation and development of the Group," and stated that:

Belén Garijo will bring an increased rigor to the implementation of Sanofi's strategy and accelerate the preparation of the Group's future. Her priority will be to strengthen the productivity, governance, and innovation capacity of Research & Development.

Sanofi stock fell in value after Q4 earnings were reported, from ~$47.5 per share to ~$45.5, quickly recovering to >$49 before today's news – at the time of writing, shares trade at ~$47 per share.

The general consensus seems to be that the outgoing Hudson has paid the price for being unable to execute a turnaround plan fast enough, and in light of pressure placed on its vaccine division, particularly in its largest market, the U.S., which has seen vaccine-skeptic Secretary Robert F. Kennedy appointed as head of the Department for Health & Human Services ("HHS").

Analysis - Has Exiting CEO Left Sanofi Swimming, Or Sinking?

The last time I posted about Sanofi for Seeking Alpha, I was covering the news that its promising pipeline asset indicated for multiple sclerosis, tolebrutinib, had missed the primary endpoint in its pivotal clinical study of patients with primary progressive multiple sclerosis and had its regulatory review in non-relapsing secondary progressive multiple sclerosis extended.

That was in December, and I also discussed the fact that the Pharma had been struggling to grow revenues and operating income for several years – 2024 revenues of $45.9bn were scarcely higher than in 2020, and operating income of $9.2bn lower than in 2020, 2021, and 2022.

I discussed a potential over-reliance on the incredible success of asthma / COPD / atopic dermatitis drug Dupixent, which earned ~€15.7bn of revenues in 2024, or ~36% of Sanofi total revenues. By my count, outside of its vaccine division, only four other drugs earned "blockbuster" (>$1bn per annum) revenues in 2025, and none of those four earned >$2bn.

In 2025, sales of Sanofi's influenza and COVID-19 vaccines were down ~9%, to €2.3bn, polio and pertussis down ~7%, to €2.6bn, and Meningitis, travel, and endemic were down ~2%, to €1.3bn.

All things considered, then, there may have been some grounds for the company opting to appoint a new leader, although I did also note in December that:

Sanofi has a portfolio of recently launched drugs with significant growth potential - Altuviio, a hemophilia drug, has been set peak revenues expectations of >$2bn per annum, Nexiazyme, indicated for Pompe disease, >$1bn, Sarclisa (multiple myeloma), potentially >$2bn, Ayvakit >$2bn in the indication of systemic mastocytosis, and so on.

I also quoted the CEO (at the time), Hudson, as telling analysts at a recent conference:

We've come a long way. Just to give you some headlines. We were, I don't know, $33 billion in revenue when I joined, around $45 billion now. We were 118,000 people in 2019. We're around about 72,000 now. We forget how many, but upwards of 70 sites in manufacturing, we're now 38. We've really tried to modernize the company, we exited consumer. We've tried to turn ourselves into an R&D led pharma company because we felt we were structurally going to be behind our peers.

Sanofi was also able to announce an agreement with the U.S. government around drug pricing in December last year, which, although the details were confidential, does include a three-year exemption from tariffs on pharma imports into the U.S.

In summary, although Sanofi clearly has work to do to bring through more blockbuster drug products, repairing damage to the vaccine division, and rejuvenating the business, it is arguably an odd decision to dismiss the CEO just when the top line appears to be rising again and when guidance for 2026 is for high-single-digit percentage sales growth and business earnings per share ("EPS") growth "slightly faster than sales". Sales growth in Q4 2025 was >13% year-on-year.

Ultimately, it may have simply been bad timing for the outgoing CEO, whose tenure was up for renewal in a couple of month's time.

Looking Ahead - Highlights To Look Out For In 2026 & Beyond

As we can see below (slide from the Q4 earnings presentation), many of Sanofi's more recently launched drugs made good progress in 2025:

With that said, it's unlikely that the peak revenue opportunity for any of these products extends much beyond the $2-3bn mark, based on analysts' expectations, at least, and with Dupixent's patents potentially expiring early next decade, that could lead to a significant drop-off in annual revenues.

Below, I have listed Sanofi's products and revenues, plus chemical name, mechanism of action, and target indication, along with a rough estimate of sales performance from 2025 to 2030:

In 2025, Sanofi's presentation states the company launched three new drugs – Qfitlia, Wayrilz, and Nuvaxovid – part of its deal with Novavax (NVAX) to license its COVID vaccine technology.

If we consider the mid-to-late stage pipeline above, there is plenty of potential; however, we must apply a discount to future revenue contributions according to the risk these products fall short in studies and do not make it to market, or even if they do, fail to achieve standard-of-care ("SoC") or best-in-class status.

I'd rate the immunology pipeline as the most promising, given the huge market sizes (>$100bn per annum, all told), but note the intense competition, with incumbents such as AbbVie (ABBV) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) hard to take market share away from. A Table of upcoming news flow is shown below:

Concluding Thoughts: New CEO Faces Tough Challenges - I'm Optimistic

Sanofi is a bona fide Pharma giant that pays an annual dividend currently yielding 3.25% (ADR shares) and with a market cap valuation of $119bn. That is low (in my opinion) for a Pharma generating such a high volume of revenues but also reflects numerous risks to the business, as I have discussed above, the most significant being the over-reliance on Dupixent, in my view, plus vaccine uncertainty.

As such, I think the news CEO faces some tricky challenges, particularly finding ~$10bn – $15bn of new revenue streams in order to keep the company growing, and potentially even more, to offset the looming patent expiry of Dupixent.

Sanofi stock is up just over 20% on a 10-year basis, but my conclusion is that even with threats to its main sources of revenue, being under new management, and with vaccine headwinds to deal with, the pipeline does look strong enough to suggest the market has been overselling stock, and on a three-to-five-year basis, I see enough potential to lift the market cap above $150bn – let's say a forward price-to-sale ratio of <3x, which is low for a "Big Pharma" company. As an additional fillip, management is promising to complete a €1bn share buyback in 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: American Express: A Steady Compounder Worth Considering (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
It has been a while, but at the end of August 2025, I reiterated my sell rating on American Express Company (AXP). In that analysis, I found that even though the company beat estimates, they failed to raise full-year guidance, and that's usually a sign of cautiousness. Furthermore, the valuation was determined to be elevated. Since the publication of that update, the stock has gone on to advance by around 9%, even after the recent pullback. Today, we'll see if this strength is driven by the fundamentals or whether American Express remains a sell.

In the below analysis, the company is shown to be one that has demonstrated resilience. Activity has remained solid in their 2025 Q4, and the excellent control of operating expenses has made profitability a highlight. Moving forward, investors can expect more of the same, but that's not a bad thing at all. Steady growth and improving profitability are outlined in their 2026 guidance. While there are some political risks, the valuation pullback has made current levels in the stock fair. I previously concluded that the stock was expensive, but considering their impressive levels of resilience, I now believe the fundamentals can justify the price tag. Therefore, I have decided to upgrade American Express to a hold, as investors should give the stock some consideration.

Key Metrics

Today, we'll start with a look at some of the company's key metrics. Total billed business is a good reflection of both American Express' business activity and also the economic confidence in the U.S. and in international markets.

For 2025 Q4, they saw totalled billed business growth of 8% YoY. As you can see above, both G&S and T&E saw 8% growth, and so there is broad resilience. While there wasn't further acceleration from Q3, I believe this is enough to show that spending is holding up okay despite a shaky consumer confidence index in various economies around the world. In fact, U.S. Consumer Services Billed Business growth of 9% outpaced the Commercial Services Billed Business YoY increase of just 3% as a signal that consumers are actually still spending.

Lastly, while transaction growth actually dipped from 10% in Q3 to 9%, the total billed business figure is usually a better reflection of overall spending strength, and so I would say activity has been solid for American Express.

While the overall business is looking solid, there may be some undesirable weakness in their new card metrics. At the end of the day, new cards and new users are a major source of growth for the company in the long term. Usually some signs of spending hesitancy show up in these figures and can translate into lower business growth down the road. As you can see above, in Q4, there were 2.9 million proprietary new cards acquired. This isn't a small number per se, but it does represent a notable drop YoY. Therefore, investors should at least be somewhat cautious.

There are some good signs, however. For example, it does seem American Express is resonating with the younger demographic since 65% of consumer new accounts were acquired by millennials or members of Gen-Z. That is important as these demographics increasingly make up the largest group of spenders.

Credit Quality

As a good sign for American Express, it seems that cardholders aren't becoming excessively delinquent. As you can see above, the percentage of card member loans and receivables that are over 30 days due has remained unchanged at 1.3%. The net write-off rate has edged up from 1.9% both YoY and QoQ, but at 2.1% it isn't extraordinarily high by any means. At the end of January, Seeking Alpha's compilation of credit card metrics showed that December delinquency and net charge-off rates drifted lower from November for the sector as a whole. While this may not be obvious in the above charts, it should be encouraging for investors that the overall macro environment seems to be heading in the right direction.

Top Line Growth Slows Modestly

For the top line, American Express reported revenues net of interest expense of $18.98 billion, which represents growth of 10% YoY or 9% in constant currency. This is indicative of resilience, but note that both the reported and constant currency growth rates are decelerations from 11% in Q3. While this is definitely not desirable, note they were still able to beat analyst expectations by a decent margin of $53.51 million.

While discount revenue growth underperformed the company-wide revenue growth rate, note that the 8% reported for Q4 was stronger than the full-year increase of 6%. Since this is American Express' most important driver for the top line, this strong momentum to end the year is a good sign. Another key metric, net interest income, saw stable results as the 12% constant currency increase is unchanged from the previous quarter. Overall, it does seem that this interest rate environment has been generally positive for them.

Profitability

In Q4 specifically, operating expenses increased by just 5%. Since this lags the top-line growth rate considerably, it indicates that business efficiency has been strong. In the above charts, you can see how this is playing out on the multiyear time frame. For 2025, total operating expenses were 22% of revenue, down from 23% in 2024. In fact, this metric is on a clear downtrend as a signal that the business is getting increasingly well run.

What I want to highlight is the fact that technology expense as a percentage of revenue has remained unchanged over the past four years. This shows the company is continuing to invest in the future of their business. In 2025, they spent $5 billion on technology, up 11% YoY and that's an acceleration from the previous year's increase. For long-term investors, this should be a great sign.

Outlook Is Resilient

American Express provided straightforward guidance for 2026. For revenue, they are expecting 9.5% growth at the midpoint, which would be a mild slowdown from 10% growth in 2025. Therefore, investors should look forward to a continuation of resilience but not extraordinary growth.

As for EPS, the midpoint of the above range implies growth of 14%. This would also be a slight dip from 2025's 15% adjusted EPS growth rate. Do note, however, that with bottom-line growth expected to exceed top-line growth, strong business efficiency is set to continue.

As you can see above, in the longer term, the company is aiming for over 10% of revenue growth and EPS growth in the mid-teens percentage range. Overall, in my view, guidance shows that it'll mostly be more of the same for American Express, but that isn't necessary a bad thing.

Valuation Has Corrected

Despite the recent contraction in the forward P/E ratio, the current reading of 20.15 is actually only slightly below the level found in my previous update. I called American Express overvalued back then, but I now believe that the stock is more or less fairly valued. The company's demonstration of stability and profitability justifies the relatively premium price tag relative to the financial sector.

Today, I analyzed a company that is seeing resilient activity, decent growth, and improving profitability. In the outlook discussion above, it is shown that American Express is likely in for more of the same for 2026. Stable growth and good business efficiency. That's not at all bad for a very mature, large financial company. In my view, the fundamentals look solid, and shareholders get to pocket a reliable dividend and see some share repurchases as well. With the multiple having corrected to around 20, I view American Express to be worth its price tag, and for investors looking for a stable compounder, this may be a stock to consider.

Political Risks

The Trump Administration has become a notable risk for American Express' business. While the likelihood that policies will actually be implemented remains low in my view, political risk is definitely something that investors should have in mind.

In early January, President Trump called for a one-year credit card interest rate cap of 10%. This clearly would have massive implications for a company like American Express, and the stock saw significant losses after this news. I believe this was a bit of an overreaction, as this policy would be difficult to enact. Putting in this rate cap will ultimately hurt consumers, as the supply of credit will more than likely drop drastically, and so the risk for American Express remains low.

On Jan 13th, the President also called for "everyone" to support Senator Roger Marshall's Credit Card Competition Act that targets swipe fees. This could affect American Express' discount revenues, and so this adds to the Trump Administration's hostile stance toward credit card companies.

Conclusion

As of now, I'm refraining from calling American Express a buy for a couple of reasons. While the valuation has come down, note that Seeking Alpha shows that the non-GAAP forward P/E ratio is still at an almost 90% premium to the financials sector, so the stock isn't anywhere near cheap. Secondly, political risks are present even if they aren't as concerning as implied by the stock's reaction. I would say at current levels, you get what you pay for, and while the stock is worth consideration for investors, I've decided to upgrade to just a hold for now.
